cards.back: Where does this person come from?
the thing of the same make and build
Pronouns used instead of a proper noun, referring to a person or thing
to become (also auxiliary for future tense)
used to show you like doing something (Ich koche gerne = I like to cook)
indicates an assumption, not a fact
in the end / finally / at last
to drink (like an animal or an alcoholic)
Foreign words ending in -o (Auto, Foto, Konto)
Zeitpunkt = point in time (um/am). Zeitraum = period (von…bis)
To contradict a negative question/assumption. "Du isst kein Fleisch?" → "Doch, ich esse Fleisch!"
Negate verbs (end of sentence), adjectives/pronouns (before them). Used with definite/possessive articles.
Used instead of indefinite article or with nouns without articles. (Hast du Hunger? → Nein, ich habe keinen Hunger.)
1) Yes/No: verb first, then subject. 2) W-question: question word first, verb second.
which (der → welcher, die → welche, das → welches, pl. → welche)
Shows the direct object — what is directly impacted by the subject
Shows the recipient of the action
to enjoy / to take pleasure in something
"The presentation effect" — things stop working when you try to show them

cards.front: When do we NOT use an article?
cards.back: Plural of indefinite, materials, non-specific amounts, jobs, abstract concepts, geographical names
ich weiß, du weißt, er weiß, wir wissen, ihr wisst, Sie wissen
archery (der Bogen = bow, der Pfeil = arrow)
Verbs combined with a prefix (usually a preposition). When conjugated, the prefix goes to the end of the sentence.
to drive → to drive off (prefix goes to end)
to shout → to call (prefix goes to end)
Add -te (der erste, der zweite, der dritte, der vierte…)
Prepositions of time — describe WHEN something happens (accusative or dative)

cards.front: How is a noun's case determined?
cards.back: 1) By function (subject, direct/indirect object) 2) By the preposition in front of it. Preposition beats function!
an unethically bred race / a race that suffers from how it's bred
to take time off for overtime
to think about something / to ponder
to strike through / to cross out

cards.front: Es hat nichts mit … zu tun

cards.back: Verbs needing a 'recipient': danken, helfen, gehören, gefallen, geben, schenken, sagen, antworten…
to turn (when taking directions) (bog ab – abgebogen sein)
All nouns ending in -ung are feminine, e.g. die Zeitung, die Wohnung
to go for a stroll (spazierte – spaziert sein)
in the middle of …, e.g. 'In der Mitte von meiner Küche ist ein Tisch.'
well maintained / in order / well organized
to move from one apartment to another (umgezogen sein)
the communal apartment / the shared apartment
I'm curious to find out / I'm excited to see what happens next
to know / be familiar with something or someone (kannte – gekannt haben)
How does it go on? / How does it continue?
to turn on a street (bog ab – abgebogen sein)
to fix / to repair (reparierte – repariert haben)
a dilapidated shed / a house that should be abandoned
to propose / to suggest (schlug vor – vorgeschlagen haben)
to cancel a meeting (sagte ab – abgesagt haben)
a set of final exams in school; passing it entitles you to study at a university
ich werde, du wirst, er wird, wir werden, ihr werdet, Sie werden
to speak on the phone (telefonierte – telefoniert haben)

cards.front: Gibt es hier Worte, die wir nicht verstehen, oder die ganz neu sind?
cards.back: Are there any words that we don't understand or that are completely new?
'mit' takes the dative, 'ohne' takes the accusative, e.g. 'Ich gehe mit dem Kind spazieren' vs. 'Ich gehe ohne das Kind spazieren'
The second verb goes to the end of the sentence and stays in the infinitive, e.g. 'Ich will ein Buch lesen.'
to stretch, as in yoga or sports (stretchte – gestretcht haben)
ich soll, du sollst, er soll, wir sollen, ihr sollt, Sie sollen
to earn / to deserve (verdiente – verdient haben)
Nadine is on her phone / Nadine is using her phone
